================================================================================================

CHICAGO MUNICIPAL CODE CHAPTER 4-6: BUSINESS LICENSING
--------------------------------------------------------

All businesses operating within the City of Chicago must obtain and maintain a valid business license issued by the City.

ng within the City of Chicago must obtain and maintain a valid business license issued by the City. Chapter 4-6 of the Chicago Municipal Code ("Business Licenses") sets forth the general licensing requirements, application procedures, renewal obligations, and the rights of licensees in enforcement proceedings.

pplication procedures, renewal obligations, and the rights of licensees in enforcement proceedings.

LICENSE TYPES: Chicago requires specific licenses for a wide range of business activities, including but not limited to: retail food establishments, restaurants, bars and taverns, limited business licenses (LBL) for general retail and service businesses, home-based businesses, contractors, amusement establishments, tobacco retailers, and many professional categories.

nesses, contractors, amusement establishments, tobacco retailers, and many professional categories. The Department of Business Affairs and Consumer Protection (BACP) administers most business license categories.

APPLICATION AND RENEWAL: License applications must be submitted with the required documentation, applicable fees, and evidence that the business premises comply with zoning ordinances, building codes, and fire prevention codes.

hat the business premises comply with zoning ordinances, building codes, and fire prevention codes. Licenses are typically issued for two-year terms and must be renewed before expiration. Chicago Municipal Code §4-6-010 and related sections set forth the general requirements.

xpiration. Chicago Municipal Code §4-6-010 and related sections set forth the general requirements.

BUSINESS OWNER RIGHTS IN LICENSE PROCEEDINGS:

License Denial: If BACP denies a license application, the applicant has the right to a hearing before the Department of Administrative Hearings (DOAH) (Chicago Municipal Code §4-4-060 et seq.). The denial notice must specify the grounds for denial.

) (Chicago Municipal Code §4-4-060 et seq.). The denial notice must specify the grounds for denial. The applicant must file a request for hearing with DOAH within the timeframe stated in the notice — typically 35 days of service.

License Revocation or Suspension: Before revoking or suspending a license, the City must provide the licensee with written notice stating the alleged violations and the date, time, and location of the administrative hearing.

tice stating the alleged violations and the date, time, and location of the administrative hearing. The hearing is conducted before an Administrative Law Officer (ALO) at DOAH. The licensee has the right to: appear in person, be represented by an attorney, present evidence and witnesses, and cross-examine City witnesses.

n, be represented by an attorney, present evidence and witnesses, and cross-examine City witnesses. The ALO issues a recommended finding, which is reviewed and finalized by the City.

License Conditions: In some cases, BACP may seek to impose conditions on a license (e.g., restricted hours of operation, required security measures, noise mitigation).

s on a license (e.g., restricted hours of operation, required security measures, noise mitigation). Conditions can also be contested through DOAH.

Appeals of DOAH Decisions: Following the DOAH final decision, a licensee may seek judicial review in the Circuit Court of Cook County (Administrative Review Law, 735 ILCS 5/3-101 et seq.).

l review in the Circuit Court of Cook County (Administrative Review Law, 735 ILCS 5/3-101 et seq.). The petition for administrative review must typically be filed within 35 days of service of the final DOAH order.

CHICAGO SIDEWALK AND INFRASTRUCTURE OBLIGATIONS
------------------------------------------------

CHICAGO SIDEWALK AND INFRASTRUCTURE OBLIGATIONS
------------------------------------------------

SNOW AND ICE REMOVAL: Under Chicago Municipal Code §10-8-180, property owners and occupants are required to remove snow and ice from abutting sidewalks within 3 inches of the property line within a specified period after snowfall ends. For public sidewalks abutting residential properties, snow must be cleared within 24 hours after snowfall stops.

dewalks abutting residential properties, snow must be cleared within 24 hours after snowfall stops. Commercial properties on major arterials may face shorter windows during declared snow emergencies.

Failure to maintain snow-free sidewalks can result in: a municipal ticket (fine of $50-$500 or more depending on circumstances); a City complaint and citation; civil liability if someone slips and falls on the property owner's portion of the sidewalk.

tation; civil liability if someone slips and falls on the property owner's portion of the sidewalk. Chicago follows the "snow and ice removal act" principles, and courts have found commercial property owners liable for injuries caused by negligently maintained sidewalks.

ve found commercial property owners liable for injuries caused by negligently maintained sidewalks.

POTHOLE AND STREET DEFECT REPORTING: Chicago 311 (call 311, use the CHI311 app, or go online at chicago.gov/311) handles reports of potholes, damaged sidewalks, broken streetlights, cave-ins, street sign issues, and other infrastructure defects. Every complaint generates a service request (SR) number for tracking.

other infrastructure defects. Every complaint generates a service request (SR) number for tracking. Chicago's 311 system integrates with the City's work order management system, and SR numbers can be used to check progress online.

CHICAGO'S 311 SYSTEM: The City of Chicago's 311 service request system is available 24/7 by phone, online at chicago.gov/311, and through the CHI311 mobile app.

st system is available 24/7 by phone, online at chicago.gov/311, and through the CHI311 mobile app. It tracks service requests across more than 100 city service categories including: pothole repair, alley repairs, graffiti removal, tree trimming, building violations, and noise complaints.

ILLINOIS SMALL CLAIMS COURT
----------------------------

Illinois provides a Small Claims Court track within the Circuit Court system for resolving money disputes without complex procedures or mandatory attorney representation.

DOLLAR LIMIT: $10,000 per claim.

without complex procedures or mandatory attorney representation.

DOLLAR LIMIT: $10,000 per claim. Claims between $10,001 and $100,000 may be filed as "civil law" cases on the regular civil docket.

WHERE TO FILE IN COOK COUNTY: File at the Circuit Court of Cook County (for Chicago and surrounding suburban municipalities). The main courthouse is located at Richard J. Daley Center, 50 W. Washington Street, Chicago, IL 60602.

main courthouse is located at Richard J. Daley Center, 50 W. Washington Street, Chicago, IL 60602. Additional courthouses are located in suburban Cook County districts (Bridgeview, Rolling Meadows, Markham, Maywood, Skokie, Waukegan for Lake County, etc.).

FILING FEES: Approximately $221-$266 for claims up to $10,000 in Cook County (fees vary slightly by court house and are subject to periodic adjustment).

$10,000 in Cook County (fees vary slightly by court house and are subject to periodic adjustment). Check the Circuit Court of Cook County's website at cookcountycourt.org for current fees.

PROCEDURE: The plaintiff (person filing) completes a Small Claims Complaint form, pays the filing fee, and the clerk issues summons to the defendant. The defendant must be served with the summons and complaint. The first hearing date is typically set 30-45 days out.

t be served with the summons and complaint. The first hearing date is typically set 30-45 days out. Both parties attend; if either party fails to appear, the case may be dismissed or default judgment entered. At the hearing, present your evidence to the judge (or magistrate) in plain language. No strict evidentiary rules apply in Small Claims Court.

WHO CAN REPRESENT WHOM: Individuals may represent themselves.

y rules apply in Small Claims Court.

WHO CAN REPRESENT WHOM: Individuals may represent themselves. Corporations, LLCs, and partnerships must technically be represented by an attorney in Illinois courts (including small claims). However, in practice, small claims courts are accessible to unrepresented business owners, and the threshold for enforcing the representation rule varies by judge.

presented business owners, and the threshold for enforcing the representation rule varies by judge.

ENFORCEMENT OF JUDGMENTS: A judgment from Small Claims Court can be enforced through: citation to discover assets (a proceeding compelling the debtor to disclose assets); wage deduction (garnishment — limited to 15% of gross wages or 25% of disposable earnings); bank account citations; and recording a judgment lien on real estate owned by the debtor in any county where the judgment is recorded.

ng a judgment lien on real estate owned by the debtor in any county where the judgment is recorded.

STATUTES OF LIMITATIONS IN ILLINOIS:
- Written contracts: 10 years (735 ILCS 5/13-206)
- Oral contracts: 5 years (735 ILCS 5/13-205)
- Personal injury: 2 years (735 ILCS 5/13-202)
- Property damage: 5 years

COMMERCIAL TENANT RIGHTS IN CHICAGO
-------------------------------------

Chicago's well-known Residential Landlord-Tenant Ordinance (RLTO), codified at Chicago Municipal Code Chapter 5-12, provides extensive protections to residential tenants — but it expressly DOES NOT APPLY to commercial rental relationships. Commercial landlords and tenants in Chicago are governed primarily by:

1. The express terms of the written lease agreement
2.

nants in Chicago are governed primarily by:

1. The express terms of the written lease agreement
2. Illinois common law of contracts
3. Illinois statutes governing commercial real estate

KEY PROTECTIONS APPLICABLE TO COMMERCIAL TENANTS:

Prohibition on Self-Help Eviction: Even though commercial tenants do not have RLTO protections, Illinois law prohibits landlords from using "self-help" eviction for commercial tenants.

otections, Illinois law prohibits landlords from using "self-help" eviction for commercial tenants. Under 735 ILCS 5/9-101 et seq., a landlord seeking to evict a commercial tenant who holds a valid lease must file a Forcible Entry and Detainer action in Circuit Court and obtain a court order and sheriff's eviction.

orcible Entry and Detainer action in Circuit Court and obtain a court order and sheriff's eviction. A landlord who changes locks, removes the tenant's property, or physically shuts off utilities to force a commercial tenant out without a court order commits an unlawful act and can face civil liability and potential criminal charges.

Lease Enforcement: Commercial tenants may sue for breach of lease in Circuit Court (Small Claims if under $10,000, regular civil docket for larger amounts).

of lease in Circuit Court (Small Claims if under $10,000, regular civil docket for larger amounts). Common grounds: landlord fails to repair what the lease requires; landlord interferes with quiet enjoyment; landlord fails to provide utilities the lease specifies.

Security Deposits: Commercial lease security deposit terms are governed entirely by the lease.

es.

Security Deposits: Commercial lease security deposit terms are governed entirely by the lease. Unlike residential deposits, there is no Illinois statute specifying return timeframes or itemization requirements for commercial security deposits. Review your lease for specific terms.

or itemization requirements for commercial security deposits. Review your lease for specific terms. If the lease is silent, courts apply general contract principles.

Triple Net Leases: Chicago commercial tenants should be aware that many commercial leases are "triple net" (NNN), requiring the tenant to pay not just base rent but also a proportionate share of real estate taxes, building insurance, and common area maintenance (CAM) costs.

portionate share of real estate taxes, building insurance, and common area maintenance (CAM) costs. Understand your total occupancy cost before signing.

Holdover Tenancies: If a commercial tenant stays beyond the lease expiration without signing a renewal, Illinois courts may treat the holdover as creating a month-to-month tenancy on the same terms as the original lease.

ay treat the holdover as creating a month-to-month tenancy on the same terms as the original lease. The landlord may also elect to treat the holdover tenant as a trespasser and initiate eviction proceedings.

CHICAGO DEPARTMENT OF BUSINESS AFFAIRS AND CONSUMER PROTECTION (BACP)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

BACP is Chicago's primary consumer protection and business licensing agency.

---------------------

BACP is Chicago's primary consumer protection and business licensing agency. It serves two roles: protecting consumers from deceptive business practices, and administering the business license system.

CONSUMER PROTECTION ENFORCEMENT: BACP enforces the Chicago Consumer Protection Ordinance (Municipal Code Chapter 2-25), which mirrors and supplements the Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act (815 ILCS 505/1 et seq.).

plements the Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act (815 ILCS 505/1 et seq.). Prohibited practices include:

- Price gouging: Unreasonably increasing prices of essential goods or services during a declared emergency
- Deceptive advertising: False or misleading statements in any advertisement or sales communication
- Bait-and-switch: Advertising a product at one price and then refusing to sell at that price

ation
- Bait-and-switch: Advertising a product at one price and then refusing to sell at that price
- Failure to honor refund policies: If a business has a posted refund policy, it must honor it
- Unconscionable contracts: Contract terms that are oppressively one-sided

FILING A CONSUMER COMPLAINT WITH BACP: Consumers can file complaints online at chicago.gov/bacp, by phone at 312-744-9400, or in person at 121 N. LaSalle Street, Room 805, Chicago, IL 60602.

bacp, by phone at 312-744-9400, or in person at 121 N. LaSalle Street, Room 805, Chicago, IL 60602. BACP will investigate and attempt mediation. If violations are substantiated, BACP can assess fines, require restitution, or refer the matter for license action.

BUSINESS-TO-BUSINESS COMPLAINTS: Business owners who believe a competitor or vendor has engaged in deceptive or illegal business practices can also file a BACP complaint.

or or vendor has engaged in deceptive or illegal business practices can also file a BACP complaint. BACP has authority over licensed businesses in Chicago and can take action against businesses that violate consumer protection laws regardless of whether the victim is a consumer or another business.

iolate consumer protection laws regardless of whether the victim is a consumer or another business.

ILLINOIS CONSUMER FRAUD ACT: In addition to BACP complaints, businesses and consumers can file a private lawsuit under the Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act (815 ILCS 505/1 et seq.) in Circuit Court. This statute allows recovery of actual damages plus attorneys' fees in successful cases.

uit Court. This statute allows recovery of actual damages plus attorneys' fees in successful cases. Claims must be filed within 3 years of the deceptive act.

LICENSE DISPUTE RESOLUTION — CHICAGO DEPARTMENT OF ADMINISTRATIVE HEARINGS (DOAH)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

EARINGS (DOAH)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

When a business faces a formal enforcement action by the City — a license revocation, suspension, denial, or administrative fine — the Chicago Department of Administrative Hearings (DOAH) is the forum for adjudication.

DOAH OVERVIEW: DOAH is an independent quasi-judicial agency that conducts hearings for most City of Chicago code violations and license actions. It is located at 400 W.

ucts hearings for most City of Chicago code violations and license actions. It is located at 400 W. Superior Street, Chicago, IL 60654. Phone: 312-742-8585.

TYPES OF CASES HEARD AT DOAH:
- Business license disputes (denial, revocation, suspension)
- Building code violations (violation notices from Department of Buildings)
- Health code violations (violation notices from Department of Public Health)
- Zoning code violations
- Municipal code violations (noise, sanitation, animals, etc.)

ic Health)
- Zoning code violations
- Municipal code violations (noise, sanitation, animals, etc.)

RIGHTS IN DOAH HEARINGS:
1. Right to notice: You must receive written notice of the hearing date, location, and the charges against you with sufficient time to prepare (typically at least 7 days).
2. Right to counsel: You may be represented by a licensed Illinois attorney at your own expense.
3.

2. Right to counsel: You may be represented by a licensed Illinois attorney at your own expense.
3. Right to present evidence: You can submit documents, photographs, receipts, contracts, and other evidence. Make multiple copies.
4. Right to call witnesses: You can call witnesses to testify on your behalf.
5. Right to cross-examine: You can question any witness the City presents.
6.

stify on your behalf.
5. Right to cross-examine: You can question any witness the City presents.
6. Right to a written decision: The Administrative Law Officer must issue a written decision explaining the basis for any finding.
7. Right to appeal: Following DOAH's final decision, you may file for Administrative Review in the Circuit Court of Cook County within 35 days.

FILING DEADLINES: The notice of hearing will specify how many days you have to request a continuance or file a defense.

e notice of hearing will specify how many days you have to request a continuance or file a defense. Missing a hearing without rescheduling can result in a default order against you.

================================================================================

MAINTAIN PREMISES
================================================================================

If your landlord materially fails to comply with the rental agreement or with
applicable building codes that materially affect health and safety, you have
the following statutory remedies:

PRECONDITION — WRITTEN NOTICE REQUIREMENT:
Before exercising most remedies, you MUST give written notice to the landlord.
The notice must:
1. State the address and unit number
2.

1. State the address and unit number
2. Describe the condition requiring repair with reasonable specificity
3. State which remedy you intend to exercise if repairs are not made
4. Be delivered to the landlord by:

   - Email if landlord has previously agreed to email notices in writing

- Standard code violations: 14 days notice

The vast majority of states require that before a person can file a civil lawsuit against a municipality, county, or other local government entity, they must first serve a formal "Notice of Claim" (also called a Government Tort Claim) on the relevant government entity.

a formal "Notice of Claim" (also called a Government Tort Claim) on the relevant government entity. This procedural prerequisite exists to give governments an opportunity to investigate claims while evidence is fresh, evaluate their exposure, and potentially settle meritorious claims without litigation.

Failure to file a timely Notice of Claim is a complete bar to the lawsuit in most jurisdictions — courts do not have discretion to overlook the omission unless a specific exception applies.

tions — courts do not have discretion to overlook the omission unless a specific exception applies. Filing deadlines are short and strictly enforced. Claimants who miss the deadline typically lose their right to sue the government entity permanently.

ACTUAL VS. CONSTRUCTIVE NOTICE OF A DEFECT
-------------------------------------------

One of the central issues in any municipal negligence case is whether the government entity had "notice" of the dangerous condition before the injury occurred.

s whether the government entity had "notice" of the dangerous condition before the injury occurred. Notice is a prerequisite to liability — a government cannot be held responsible for a defect it did not know about and could not reasonably have discovered.

ACTUAL NOTICE: Actual notice exists when the government entity had direct, firsthand knowledge of the defect.

ICE: Actual notice exists when the government entity had direct, firsthand knowledge of the defect. Examples include: a DOT worker who personally inspected and documented a pothole; an internal report documenting a dangerous intersection; a prior repair attempt that was inadequate.

CONSTRUCTIVE NOTICE: Constructive notice exists when a condition has been present for a sufficient period of time that a reasonably vigilant municipality should have discovered it through routine inspection.

time that a reasonably vigilant municipality should have discovered it through routine inspection. The test is whether the defect would have been discovered with reasonable care. Courts look at: how long the condition existed; whether it was visible and obvious; whether the municipality had a regular inspection program; and whether similar conditions nearby had been noticed.

GOVERNMENTAL IMMUNITY AND ITS EXCEPTIONS
-----------------------------------------

SOVEREIGN IMMUNITY BASICS: Historically, governments could not be sued without their consent, under the doctrine of sovereign immunity ("the king can do no wrong").

sued without their consent, under the doctrine of sovereign immunity ("the king can do no wrong"). This doctrine has been substantially watered down at the federal, state, and local levels by legislation, but important immunities remain.

FEDERAL SOVEREIGN IMMUNITY: The United States government cannot be sued without Congressional consent.

ERAL SOVEREIGN IMMUNITY: The United States government cannot be sued without Congressional consent. The Federal Tort Claims Act (FTCA), 28 USC §2671 et seq., waives sovereign immunity for certain claims of personal injury caused by negligent or wrongful acts of federal employees acting within the scope of their employment. The FTCA requires administrative claim filing before suing (Standard Form 95, filed with the relevant federal agency), within 2 years of accrual.

before suing (Standard Form 95, filed with the relevant federal agency), within 2 years of accrual. If rejected, the claimant has 6 months to file in federal court.

STATE AND LOCAL IMMUNITY: Most states have enacted tort claims acts or similar statutes that partially waive immunity and allow suits against state and local governments under specified circumstances.

MINISTERIAL vs. DISCRETIONARY FUNCTIONS:
The most important immunity exception in state law concerns the distinction between ministerial and discretionary government acts:
- Discretionary functions: High-level policy decisions involving the exercise of judgment and planning. Governments typically retain immunity for discretionary functions.

ercise of judgment and planning. Governments typically retain immunity for discretionary functions. Examples: deciding which neighborhoods to prioritize for road resurfacing; adopting a particular engineering design for an intersection; policy decisions about traffic light placement.
- Ministerial functions: Day-to-day operational tasks with no policy component — tasks the government is required to perform in a specified way. Governments generally do NOT have immunity for ministerial functions.

o perform in a specified way. Governments generally do NOT have immunity for ministerial functions. Examples: actually repairing a pothole after a work order was issued; snow removal from a roadway on a schedule mandated by ordinance; maintaining a traffic signal in working order.

Most states waive immunity for maintenance of public property (ministerial), but retain it for design decisions (discretionary).

r maintenance of public property (ministerial), but retain it for design decisions (discretionary). This means: if the government decided (discretionary) to build a road with a particular drainage design that later caused flooding, it may be immune. But if the government failed to maintain (ministerial) a functional drain that caused flooding, it is not immune.

NUISANCE LAW: FUNDAMENTALS
----------------------------

Nuisance is a broad category of tort law that addresses unreasonable interference with the use and enjoyment of property. There are two fundamental types:

PUBLIC NUISANCE: A public nuisance is an unreasonable interference with a right held in common by the general public.

public nuisance is an unreasonable interference with a right held in common by the general public. Examples include: excessive noise from a commercial establishment audible to an entire neighborhood, air pollution, illegal dumping on public property, obstruction of public highways.

re neighborhood, air pollution, illegal dumping on public property, obstruction of public highways. A public nuisance is typically addressed through government action (code enforcement, civil injunction brought by a municipality or state attorney general), though private individuals who suffer special harm distinct from the general public may also have a private cause of action.

s who suffer special harm distinct from the general public may also have a private cause of action.

PRIVATE NUISANCE: A private nuisance is an unreasonable, substantial interference with a plaintiff's use and enjoyment of their specific property (Restatement (Second) of Torts §822). To establish a private nuisance claim, a plaintiff must show:
1. The defendant's conduct interfered with the plaintiff's use and enjoyment of their property
2.

:
1. The defendant's conduct interfered with the plaintiff's use and enjoyment of their property
2. The interference was substantial — it caused significant harm, not merely a trivial annoyance
3. The interference was unreasonable — assessed by weighing the gravity of the harm against the social utility of the defendant's conduct, the character of the neighborhood, and whether the plaintiff is hypersensitive

efendant's conduct, the character of the neighborhood, and whether the plaintiff is hypersensitive

REMEDIES FOR NUISANCE:
- Injunction: A court order requiring the defendant to stop, reduce, or modify the activity causing the nuisance. For ongoing noise or pollution that causes continuing harm, an injunction is often the most effective remedy.

g noise or pollution that causes continuing harm, an injunction is often the most effective remedy.
- Compensatory damages: Monetary compensation for the harm already suffered, including: reduction in property value, lost rental income, out-of-pocket costs (e.g., soundproofing expenses), and general damages for loss of enjoyment.
- Abatement: In some jurisdictions, the plaintiff may be entitled to physically abate (eliminate) the nuisance under court authority.

, the plaintiff may be entitled to physically abate (eliminate) the nuisance under court authority.

KEY ELEMENTS COURTS EXAMINE IN NOISE NUISANCE CASES:
- Decibel level and frequency of the noise compared to ambient levels in the area
- Time of day the noise occurs (nighttime noise is evaluated more strictly)
- Duration and frequency of the disturbance
- Zoning district (industrial zones tolerate higher noise levels than residential zones)

isturbance

- Whether the plaintiff has given written notice to the defendant and the defendant has failed to remedy the problem

NYC ADMIN CODE §7-210: SIDEWALK LIABILITY FOR ADJACENT PROPERTY OWNERS
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

Prior to 2003, New York City was responsible for maintaining and repairing all public sidewalks within city limits.

ew York City was responsible for maintaining and repairing all public sidewalks within city limits. The Sidewalk Law, enacted as part of Local Law 49 of 2003 and codified at New York City Administrative Code §7-210, fundamentally shifted this responsibility.

nd codified at New York City Administrative Code §7-210, fundamentally shifted this responsibility. Since the law's effective date of September 14, 2003, the burden of maintaining, repairing, and replacing sidewalk flags adjacent to private property has fallen upon the owners of that property — not the City.

Under §7-210, property owners are liable in civil damages for any personal injury proximately caused by their failure to maintain the sidewalk in a reasonably safe condition.

injury proximately caused by their failure to maintain the sidewalk in a reasonably safe condition. This means that if a pedestrian trips on a cracked, raised, or otherwise defective sidewalk flag directly adjacent to a privately owned property, the property owner — not the City of New York — is the proper defendant in a personal injury lawsuit.

RESIDENTIAL vs. COMMERCIAL DISTINCTIONS:

The law carves out a partial exemption for certain residential property owners.

CIAL DISTINCTIONS:

The law carves out a partial exemption for certain residential property owners. USE OF FORCE STANDARDS
================================================================================

CONSTITUTIONAL STANDARD — Graham v. Connor, 490 U.S.

=============================================

CONSTITUTIONAL STANDARD — Graham v. Connor, 490 U.S. 386 (1989):
The Supreme Court held that all claims of excessive force by law enforcement
officers are analyzed under the Fourth Amendment's "objective reasonableness"
standard.

OBJECTIVE REASONABLENESS TEST:
A court evaluates whether the force used was objectively reasonable from the
perspective of a reasonable officer on the scene, considering:
1. The severity of the crime at issue
2.

pective of a reasonable officer on the scene, considering:
1. The severity of the crime at issue
2. Whether the subject posed an immediate threat to the safety of officers
   or others
3. Whether the subject was actively resisting arrest or attempting to evade
   arrest by flight

IMPORTANT: The officer's subjective intent or good faith does not determine
constitutionality — only whether the force was objectively reasonable.

ood faith does not determine
constitutionality — only whether the force was objectively reasonable.

EXCESSIVE FORCE:
Factors indicating excessive force:
- Continued force after suspect is restrained or subdued
- Force against compliant, non-threatening individuals
- Force that is grossly disproportionate to the situation
- Failure to give warnings before using force when feasible
- Use of deadly force against non-violent suspects

DEADLY FORCE STANDARD — Tennessee v. Garner, 471 U.S.

of deadly force against non-violent suspects

DEADLY FORCE STANDARD — Tennessee v. Garner, 471 U.S. 1 (1985):
Police may use deadly force ONLY if the officer:
1. Has probable cause to believe the suspect poses a significant threat of
   death or serious physical injury to the officer or others, AND
2.

poses a significant threat of
   death or serious physical injury to the officer or others, AND
2. Other means of apprehension are inadequate

Deadly force CANNOT be used solely to prevent a fleeing felon's escape
unless the person poses a danger to others.
